title: Staphylococcus epidermidis
body_html:
Background
Staphylococcus epidermidis is a Gram-positive, catalase positive, coagulase negative, cocci that is a facultative anaerobe. Nearly all healthy individuals carry S. epidermidis on their skin and mucous membranes.

CharacteristicDescription
Gram stainGram-positive
MorphologyNon-motile cocci arranged in grape-like clusters
Catalase testPositive
Coagulase testNegative
Oxygen requirementsFacultative anaerobe (can grow under both aerobic and anaerobic conditions)
Colony characteristicsForms white, non-hemolytic colonies on blood agar
HabitatPart of the normal human skin flora, especially in moist areas like the axillae and groin
Mode of transmissionOpportunistic: enters the body via breached skin, particularly through:
  • Indwelling medical devices (catheters, prosthetics)
  • Surgical wounds
Not spread by Airborne transmission, ingestion, or person-to-person in healthy individuals

Clinical relevance
Although typically a harmless skin commensal, Staphylococcus epidermidis is a significant opportunistic pathogen, particularly in healthcare environments.

Staphylococcus epidermidis is commonly implicated in device-associated infections, it is a leading cause of infections linked to indwelling devices like intravenous catheters, prosthetic joints, and heart valves. Staphylococcus epidermidis has the ability to form robust biofilms on medical devices, which confer resistance to antibiotics and immune clearance.

Management
Treatment of S. epidermidis infections is often complicated due to antibiotic resistance and biofilm-related persistence. Many strains are methicillin-resistant (MRSE), limiting beta-lactam use.

First-line treatment: Vancomycin is the empiric choice for suspected MRSE infections.
Alternative agents: Linezolid, daptomycin, or teicoplanin may be used based on sensitivities and clinical context.
Device removal: In many cases, removal of the colonized or infected prosthetic device is essential for successful treatment.
